title: 🤰 PREECLAMPSIA VS GESTATIONAL HTN
content:
Hypertension after 20 weeks is a major Canadian exam topic.

FeatureGestational HTNPreeclampsia
BP> 140/90> 140/90
ProteinuriaNonePositive (or organ failure)
EdemaCommonSignificant

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the definition of Preeclampsia no longer strictly requires proteinuria if there is End-organ dysfunction (low platelets, high Cr, or visual symptoms). If BP is > 160/110, it is Severe!

quiz.q: What is the only definitive treatment for severe preeclampsia?
quiz.a: Delivery of the fetus and placenta.

title: 🩸 POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH)
content:
The 4 T's mnemonic is the gold standard for MCCQE1.

1. Tone: Uterine Atony (Most common - 80%).
2. Tissue: Retained Products.
3. Trauma: Lacerations.
4. Thrombin: Coagulopathy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step for a boggy uterus is Uterine Massage and Oxytocin. If the uterus is firm but bleeding continues, the answer is Trauma/Laceration.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of PPH?

title: ⚖️ MAID (MEDICAL ASSISTANCE IN DYING)
content:
Specific to Canadian law. Two clinicians must confirm eligibility.

• Criteria: 18+ years, capable of decision, grievous and irremediable medical condition.
• Process: Must be informed of palliative care options first.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You cannot suggest MAID to a patient out of the blue. The patient must initiate the request. Also, clinicians have the right to conscientious objection but must provide a 'formal referral' in many provinces.

quiz.q: Can a person request MAID through an advance directive in Canada currently?
quiz.a: No, the patient must have capacity at the time of the request (current law).

title: ⚖️ INFORMED CONSENT
content:
Consent must be voluntary, capable, specific, and informed.

• Disclosure: You must disclose what a 'reasonable person' in the patient's position would want to know.
• Emergency: If no SDM is available and it's life-saving, you treat without consent.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A signed consent form is evidence of consent, but not consent itself. If the patient changes their mind verbally after signing, the verbal refusal trumps the form.

quiz.q: What is the age of majority for medical consent in Ontario?
quiz.a: There is no fixed age (Mature Minor doctrine applies).

title: 🧠 DEPRESSION VS ADJUSTMENT DISORDER
content:
Distinguishing the two depends on the time and severity.

FeatureAdjustment DisorderMajor Depression (MDD)
TriggerIdentifiable stressorMay or may not exist
TimingWithin 3 months of stress> 2 weeks
CriteriaSub-threshold for MDDFull SIGECAPS (5/9)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Grief (bereavement) is normal. It becomes MDD only if it's persistent, causes severe functional impairment, or involves suicidality/worthlessness.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for Adjustment Disorder?

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA TIME-LINE
content:
Psychosis is a spectrum based on duration.

• < 1 Month: Brief Psychotic Disorder.
• 1 - 6 Months: Schizophreniform.
• > 6 Months: Schizophrenia.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If mood symptoms (Depression/Mania) are present, it's Schizoaffective only if psychosis persists for 2 weeks WITHOUT mood symptoms.

quiz.q: What is the main side effect of Clozapine that requires mandatory blood monitoring?

title: 👶 DEVELOPMENTAL RED FLAGS
content:
Must-know milestones for the MCCQE1.

• 6 Months: Not sitting with support.
• 9 Months: No pincer grasp.
• 12 Months: No babbling or pointing.
• 18 Months: Not walking or < 15 words.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Loss of previously acquired skills (Regression) is the biggest red flag and requires urgent workup for metabolic or neurodegenerative disease.

quiz.q: At what age should a child normally sit without support?

title: 🧠 PEDIATRIC MENINGITIS
content:
Pathogens change by age. Treatment must be rapid.

AgePathogenTx
NewbornGBS, E. coli, ListeriaAmp + Gent/Cefotaxime
> 1 MonthS. pneumo, N. meningitidisCeftriaxone + Vanco

⚠️ Exam Trap: Kernig and Brudzinski signs are often absent in infants. Bulging fontanelle and irritability are more common clues in neonates.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of neonatal meningitis?

title: 🧬 CANCER SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
High-yield summary of Task Force guidelines.

• Cervical: Ages 25-65 (Cytology every 3 years).
• Breast: Ages 50-74 (Mammogram every 2 years).
• Colon: Ages 50-74 (FIT/FOBT every 2 years or Colonoscopy every 10).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, we generally do NOT screen for Prostate cancer with PSA routinely unless the patient requests it after a 'Shared Decision Making' discussion.

quiz.q: When do you stop screening for cervical cancer in Canada?
quiz.a: At age 65 if the last 3 results were negative.

title: 🧓 FALLS IN ELDERLY (IS HAME)
content:
A systematic approach to a falling patient.

• I: Infection (UTI/Pneumonia).
• S: Situational (Loose rugs).
• H: Hematologic (Anemia).
• A: Activity/Arrythmia.
• M: Meds (Benzos/Antihypertensives).
• E: Electrolytes.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The best predictor of a future fall is a history of a previous fall. Always check 'Get up and Go' test.

quiz.q: What is a common medication class that increases fall risk in the elderly?

title: 🧠 GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (GCA)
content:
Emergency! Risk of Permanent Blindness.

• Symptoms: New headache, Scalp tenderness, Jaw Claudication (pain with chewing).
• Lab: ESR > 50 mm/hr (High sensitivity).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If suspicion is high, start High-dose Steroids IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for the Temporal Artery Biopsy results!

quiz.q: What is the most common serious complication of GCA?

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC VS NEPHRITIC
content:
FeatureNephroticNephritic
Proteinuria> 3.5 g/dayVariable
HematuriaAbsentRBC Casts
EdemaSevereMild

⚠️ Exam Trap: Minimal Change Disease is the #1 cause in kids (Tx: Steroids). In adults, think Membranous or DM. RBC Casts = Nephritis!

quiz.q: What is the hallmark finding of a nephritic syndrome on urinalysis?

title: 🧩 INTUSSUSCEPTION
content:
Telescoping of bowel, usually in children 6-36 months.

• Triad: Colicky pain, Sausage-shaped mass, Currant Jelly Stool.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The diagnosis and initial treatment are the same: Air or Contrast Enema (Radiographic reduction).

quiz.q: What is the classic stool appearance in intussusception?

title: 🦠 PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)
content:
Infection of the upper genital tract.
• Signs: Lower abdominal pain + Cervical Motion Tenderness (Chandelier sign).
• Complication: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis (Liver capsule inflammation).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment must be Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole. You must also treat the partner to prevent re-infection!

quiz.q: What is a long-term complication of untreated PID?
quiz.a: Infertility or Ectopic Pregnancy (due to tubal scarring).

title: ❄️ HYPOTHERMIA ECG
content:
When core temp is < 35°C.
• Look for: Osborn Waves (J-waves).
• They are positive deflections at the end of the QRS complex.


⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient is not dead until they are Warm and Dead. Continue resuscitation until re-warmed to 32-35°C.

quiz.q: At what core temperature is a person defined as hypothermic?

title: 🧠 SIGECAPS (DEPRESSION)
content:
Must have 5 out of 9 for ≥ 2 weeks (including low mood or anhedonia).

S: Sleep (High/Low)
I: Interest (Loss)
G: Guilt
E: Energy (Low)
C: Concentration
A: Appetite (High/Low)
P: Psychomotor agitation/retardation
S: Suicidal ideation

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always screen for MANIA before starting SSRIs to avoid triggering a bipolar switch.

quiz.q: How long must symptoms be present to diagnose MDD?

title: 🦴 SEPTIC ARTHRITIS
content:
Medical emergency! Usually S. aureus.
• Presentation: Hot, swollen, exquisitely tender joint + Fever.
• Joint Fluid: WBC > 50,000 (Predominantly Neutrophils).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step is Arthrocentesis (Aspiration) before starting antibiotics! Antibiotics in the joint will make the culture negative.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of septic arthritis in young sexually active adults?

title: 🦋 THYROID STORM
content:
Life-threatening thyrotoxicosis.
• Signs: Fever > 40°C, Tachycardia, Agitation, Heart Failure.
• Tx: 4 P's: Propranolol, PTU, Prednisone, Potassium Iodide.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Give PTU (or Methimazole) before giving Iodine. If you give Iodine first, it will act as fuel for more hormone synthesis!

quiz.q: Why is Propranolol used in thyroid storm?
quiz.a: To control heart rate and block peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.

title: 🤰 MAGNESIUM SULFATE TOXICITY
content:
Used for neuroprotection and eclampsia prophylaxis.

SignMg Level
Loss of DTRsFirst sign
Resp. DepressionLate/Severe
Cardiac ArrestVery High

⚠️ Exam Trap: The antidote for Mg toxicity is Calcium Gluconate. Always check reflexes and urine output while on Mg.

quiz.q: What is the first physical sign of Magnesium toxicity?

title: 🧂 SIADH VS DI
content:
FeatureSIADHDiabetes Insipidus
Urine OsmHigh (>100)Low (<100)
Serum SodiumLow (Hyponatremia)High (Hypernatremia)
TxFluid RestrictDesmopressin (Central)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Small Cell Lung Cancer is a classic trigger for SIADH. Lithium is a classic trigger for Nephrogenic DI!

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for SIADH?

title: 🟡 PANCREATIC CANCER
content:
Key Sign: Painless Jaundice + Weight loss.

• Courvoisier Sign: Palpable, non-tender, enlarged gallbladder.
• Trousseau Sign: Migratory thrombophlebitis.
• Dx: CT Scan (Initial), CA 19-9 (Marker for monitoring).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In MCCQE1, if you see 'Painless Jaundice' in an elderly smoker, the answer is Pancreatic Cancer until proven otherwise. Do not wait for labs!

quiz.q: What is the most common location for pancreatic cancer?

title: ⚡ CHADS-65 (ATRIAL FIBRILLATION)
content:
Canadian Specific Algorithm for anticoagulation in AFib.

CriteriaPoints
C: Congestive Heart Failure1
H: Hypertension1
A: Age ≥ 65Mandatory OAC
D: Diabetes1
S: Stroke/TIA history1

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, if the patient is ≥ 65 years old, they automatically need an Oral Anticoagulant (OAC) regardless of other scores! DOACs (Apixaban, etc.) are preferred over Warfarin.

quiz.q: When is Warfarin preferred over DOACs in AFib?
quiz.a: Valvular AFib (Mechanical heart valves or Moderate-Severe Mitral Stenosis).

title: 🤰 METHOTREXATE FOR ECTOPIC
content:
Medical management criteria for stable ectopic pregnancy.

Absolute Contraindications:
• Hemodynamic instability (Rupture).
• IUP (Intrauterine pregnancy) present.
• Breastfeeding.

RequirementCut-off
Fetal Heart ToneAbsent
Mass Size< 3.5 - 4 cm
β-hCG Level< 5000 IU/L

⚠️ Exam Trap: If β-hCG is > 5000 or mass is large, MTX failure rate is high. The correct answer in these cases is Laparoscopic Salpingostomy/Salpingectomy.

quiz.q: What must be avoided for 72 hours after Methotrexate administration?
quiz.a: Alcohol and Folic Acid (to prevent interference with the drug).

title: 🦋 THYROID UPTAKE PATTERNS
content:
Differentiating causes of Hyperthyroidism using Radioiodine (RAIU).

PatternDiagnosis
Diffuse High UptakeGrave's Disease
Patchy/Nodal HighToxic Multinodular Goiter
Low/Zero UptakeThyroiditis or Factitious

⚠️ Exam Trap: De Quervain’s (Subacute) Thyroiditis presents with a Tender/Painful thyroid and Low uptake. Grave's is never painful!

quiz.q: What is the most likely diagnosis for a patient with hyperthyroid labs, low uptake, and a painful neck?

title: 👁️ ACUTE ANGLE CLOSURE GLAUCOMA
content:
Vision-threatening emergency.
• Symptoms: Sudden eye pain, Halos around lights, Nausea/Vomiting.
• Exam: Mid-dilated fixed pupil, 'Steamy' cornea, Rock-hard eye.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Atropine or any dilating drops! They will worsen the angle closure. Treatment includes Timolol, Pilocarpine, and Acetazolamide.

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for acute angle closure glaucoma?

title: 🦠 SYPHILIS STAGES
content:
Caused by Treponema pallidum.

• Primary: Painless Chancre.
• Secondary: Rash (Palms/Soles), Condyloma lata.
• Tertiary: Gummas, Tabes dorsalis, Argyll Robertson pupil.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Primary Syphilis, Darkfield microscopy is the fastest diagnosis. VDRL/RPR can be false negative in the first few weeks!

quiz.q: What is the gold standard treatment for all stages of Syphilis?

title: 🩸 MENTZER INDEX
content:
Differentiating Iron Deficiency from Thalassemia in microcytic anemia.

MCV / RBC count

• > 13: Likely Iron Deficiency.
• < 13: Likely Thalassemia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Thalassemia, the RBC count is often High (the body overproduces small cells), while in Iron Deficiency, the RBC count is Low.

quiz.q: What is the next best test to confirm Iron Deficiency Anemia?

title: 💓 STILL'S MURMUR (BENIGN)
content:
The most common innocent murmur in children.

• Sound: Musical, vibratory, mid-systolic.
• Location: LLSB (Lower left sternal border).
• Position: Louder when Supine (lying down).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Benign murmurs decrease in intensity when the child stands up. If the murmur gets louder with standing, think HOCM!

quiz.q: At what age is Still's murmur most commonly heard?

title: 👃 EPISTAXIS (NOSEBLEED)
content:
90% are Anterior (Kiesselbach’s Plexus).

Management Steps:
1. Blow nose + Continuous pressure (15 min).
2. Topical vasoconstrictor (Oxymetazoline).
3. Silver Nitrate cautery (if source visible).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If bleeding is bilateral, massive, or goes down the throat, think Posterior Bleed (Sphenopalatine artery). This needs Posterior Packing and mandatory Admission (risk of apnea).

quiz.q: What is the most common site for anterior epistaxis?

title: 🦶 OTTAWA ANKLE RULES
content:
A high-yield tool to avoid unnecessary X-rays.

X-ray is only needed if:
1. Bone tenderness at Posterior Edge of malleolus (last 6cm).
2. Bone tenderness at Navicular or 5th Metatarsal.
3. Inability to weight bear (4 steps).


⚠️ Exam Trap: The Ottawa rules are highly Sensitive (99%). If they are negative, you can safely rule out a fracture without an X-ray.

quiz.q: Do Ottawa Ankle Rules apply to patients under age 5?
quiz.a: No, they are generally validated for patients > 5 years old.

title: ✋ OA VS RA (HAND DIFFERENTIATION)
content:
A classic Canadian exam comparison for joint pain.

FeatureOsteoarthritis (OA)Rheumatoid (RA)
Stiffness< 30 min (Morning)> 60 min
JointsDIP / PIP (Bouchard/Heberden)MCP / PIP (Symmetry)
Ulnar DriftAbsentPresent

SVG Visual: Hand Joints

⚠️ Exam Trap: RA never affects the DIP joints. If the question mentions pain in the DIPs (fingertips), the answer is OA or Psoriatic Arthritis, not RA!

quiz.q: Which hand joint is characteristically spared in Rheumatoid Arthritis?

title: 🩸 ANEMIA LAB INTERPRETATION
content:
Sorting microcytic anemia using Ferritin and TIBC.

LabIron DeficiencyChronic Disease
FerritinLow (< 30)Normal / High
TIBCHighLow
% SaturationVery LowLow

⚠️ Exam Trap: Ferritin is an Acute Phase Reactant. In a patient with inflammation (like RA or infection), a 'Normal' ferritin doesn't rule out iron deficiency. Look for the Soluble Transferrin Receptor if available!

quiz.q: What is the single most specific test for iron deficiency anemia?

title: 🤰 GBS SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Group B Streptococcus (GBS) management in pregnancy.

• Screening: All women at 35-37 weeks (Vaginal-rectal swab).
• Treatment: Intrapartum IV Penicillin G (or Ampicillin).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a woman has GBS Bacteriuria (any concentration) during the current pregnancy, she automatically gets IV antibiotics during labor. No swab needed at 35-37 weeks!

quiz.q: What is the first-line antibiotic for GBS prophylaxis during labor?

title: 🌬️ PFT INTERPRETATION (OBSTRUCTIVE)
content:
Using the FEV1/FVC ratio to diagnose lung disease.

• Obstructive (Asthma/COPD): FEV1/FVC < 0.70.
• Restrictive (Fibrosis): FEV1/FVC is Normal or High (Both low, but ratio stays same).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Asthma, the obstruction is Reversible (>12% increase in FEV1 after bronchodilator). In COPD, the obstruction is NOT fully reversible.

quiz.q: What FEV1/FVC ratio cut-off defines an obstructive lung pattern?

title: 🧠 ANTIPSYCHOTIC METABOLIC MONITORING
content:
Second-generation antipsychotics (SGA) and metabolic syndrome.

• Highest Risk: Clozapine and Olanzapine (Weight gain/Diabetes).
• Lowest Risk: Aripiprazole and Ziprasidone.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient on Olanzapine gains 5kg in a month, the next step is NOT to stop the drug immediately, but to monitor Fasting Glucose and Lipid profile and consider switching.

quiz.q: Which two antipsychotics have the highest risk for weight gain and diabetes?

title: ⚖️ MEDICAL RECORDS OWNERSHIP
content:
A very common ethics question in Canada.

• Who owns the record? The Physician or the Hospital (The physical/digital file).
• Who owns the information? The Patient.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient has a legal right to Access and Copy their records, but they cannot take the Original file. You can charge a 'reasonable fee' for copying.

quiz.q: Can a physician withhold medical records because the patient hasn't paid their bill?
quiz.a: No, this is considered professional misconduct.

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (KIDS)
content:
Most common cause in children: Minimal Change Disease.

• Classic Presentation: Periorbital edema (worse in morning), Scrotal edema, 4+ Proteinuria.
• Treatment: Oral Corticosteroids (Prednisone).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In kids, we do NOT biopsy first. We give steroids. If they don't respond after 8 weeks (Steroid-resistant), then we do the Renal Biopsy.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children?

title: 🧂 ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (ADDISON'S)
content:
FeaturePrimary (Addison's)Secondary (Pituitary)
SkinHyperpigmentationPale skin
AldosteroneDeficient (Low Na/High K)Normal
ACTH LevelHighLow

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hyperpigmentation (bronzing) only occurs in Primary adrenal insufficiency because high ACTH cross-reacts with melanin receptors.

quiz.q: What is the screening test for Adrenal Insufficiency?
quiz.a: Standard-dose (250 mcg) ACTH Stimulation Test (Cosyntropin test).

title: 🔪 ACUTE MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
The 'Heart Attack' of the bowel. Usually due to AFib or Embolism.

• Classic Buzzword: "Pain out of proportion" to physical exam findings (Abdomen is soft but patient is screaming).
• Dx: CT Angiography.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the question mentions Metabolic Acidosis and high Lactate, the bowel is already dying (Infarction). This is a surgical emergency!

quiz.q: What cardiac condition is frequently associated with mesenteric embolism?

title: ⚡ HYPERCALCEMIA MANAGEMENT
content:
"Stones, Bones, Groans, and Psychiatric Overtones".

Management Steps:
1. Aggressive IV Fluids (Normal Saline) - Always the first step!
2. Bisphosphonates (Pamidronate/Zoledronate).
3. Calcitonin (for rapid, short-term effect).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Thiazide diuretics in hypercalcemia because they increase calcium reabsorption! Use Loop diuretics (Furosemide) only AFTER the patient is rehydrated.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients?

title: 🚫 SUICIDE RISK ASSESSMENT
content:
A mandatory check for every psychiatric presentation in Canada.

Risk Factors (SAD PERSONS):
• S: Sex (Male).
• A: Age (Young/Elderly).
• D: Depression.
• P: Previous Attempt (#1 Factor).
• E: Ethanol/Drugs.
• R: Rational loss.
• S: Sickness (Chronic).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has an Active Plan and Intent, they cannot leave the ED. If they refuse, you must Form them (Involuntary Admission) for their safety. Privacy is secondary to life!

quiz.q: What is the single strongest predictor of a completed suicide?
quiz.a: A history of previous suicide attempts.

title: 🦵 PERTHES VS SCFE (HIP PAIN)
content:
Sorting the limping child.

FeatureLegg-Calve-PerthesSlipped Capital (SCFE)
Age4 - 10 years10 - 16 years
WeightNormal/ThinObese / Overweight
PathologyAvascular NecrosisGrowth Plate Slip

SVG Visual: SCFE Slip

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hip pathology often presents as isolated knee pain (Referred pain). If an obese 13-year-old complains of knee pain, the first step is a Hip X-ray (AP and Frog-leg).

quiz.q: What is the initial management for a confirmed SCFE?
quiz.a: Non-weight bearing (Crutches) and immediate Surgical consultation for pinning.

title: 🫀 INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS (DUKE)
content:
Diagnosis requires 2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor criteria.

• Major: 2+ Positive blood cultures (Typical organisms), ECHO showing vegetation.
• Minor: Fever, IV drug use, Janeway lesions (painless), Osler nodes (painful).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If endocarditis is suspected, you must draw 3 sets of blood cultures from different sites BEFORE starting any antibiotics!

quiz.q: What is the most common organism in subacute endocarditis on a damaged valve?

title: 👶 BIOPHYSICAL PROFILE (BPP)
content:
Assessing fetal well-being when NST is non-reactive.

5 Components (2 points each):
1. NST: Reactive.
2. Breathing: ≥ 1 episode (>30 sec).
3. Movement: ≥ 3 discrete body movements.
4. Tone: ≥ 1 episode of extension/flexion.
5. AFV: At least one pocket 2x2 cm.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A score of 8/10 or 10/10 is normal. A score of 4/10 or less indicates fetal distress and usually requires Urgent Delivery!

quiz.q: What does a low amniotic fluid volume (AFV) usually indicate in the BPP?

title: ⚖️ FITNESS TO DRIVE (CANADA)
content:
Physicians have a legal duty to report patients who are unfit to drive due to medical conditions.

• Conditions: Uncontrolled Epilepsy, Severe Dementia, Substance Abuse, Sleep Apnea (with daytime sleepiness).
• Who to report: The Provincial Ministry of Transportation (e.g., MTO in Ontario).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do not need patient consent to report. Public safety overrides patient confidentiality in this scenario. You should inform the patient that you are reporting them, but you must proceed regardless of their opinion.

quiz.q: How long must a patient be seizure-free before they can usually return to driving in most provinces?

title: 🦋 ANTITHYROID DRUGS SIDE EFFECTS
content:
Methimazole and PTU (Propylthiouracil).

• Most Dangerous: Agranulocytosis (Sudden drop in WBCs).
• Symptoms: Fever, Sore throat, Mouth ulcers.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient on Methimazole develops a fever or sore throat, the next step is IMMEDIATE CBC and stopping the drug. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen!

quiz.q: Which antithyroid drug is preferred in the FIRST trimester of pregnancy?
quiz.a: PTU (due to lower risk of teratogenicity/aplasia cutis).

title: 🧱 DIVERTICULITIS MANAGEMENT
content:
Left Lower Quadrant (LLQ) pain + Fever.

• Dx: CT Scan with contrast (shows fat stranding/thickening).
• Tx (Uncomplicated): Bowel rest + Oral Antibiotics (Cipro/Metronidazole).
• Tx (Complicated/Abscess): IV Abx + Percutaneous drainage.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Colonoscopy or Barium Enema during the acute phase of diverticulitis because of the high risk of Perforation. Wait 6 weeks!

quiz.q: What is the most common complication of diverticulitis?

title: 🧂 HYPERNATREMIA CORRECTION
content:
Sodium > 145 mmol/L (usually due to dehydration).

• Risk: Correcting too fast causes Cerebral Edema.
• Rate: Do not exceed 0.5 mmol/L per hour (Max 10-12 mmol/day).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Use D5W or 0.45% Saline (Hypotonic fluids) to correct hypernatremia. If the patient is hypovolemic (Low BP), give Normal Saline (0.9%) first to stabilize vitals, then switch to hypotonic.

quiz.q: What is the main danger of correcting chronic hypernatremia too rapidly?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM VS VALPROATE
content:
First-line mood stabilizers.

• Lithium: Best for suicidal ideation/classic mania. (Risk: Thyroid/Renal).
• Valproate: Best for rapid cycling/mixed episodes. (Risk: Liver/PCOS).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Valproate is the most teratogenic (Neural Tube Defects). In a young woman of child-bearing age with Bipolar, Lithium or Lamotrigine are generally safer choices than Valproate.

quiz.q: What test must be performed before starting a patient on Lithium?
quiz.a: Creatinine (Renal function), TSH (Thyroid), and Pregnancy test.

title: 💓 PATHOLOGIC MURMURS IN KIDS
content:
How to spot a 'Bad' murmur.

Innocent (Benign)Pathologic (Abnormal)
Soft / Grade < 3Loud / Grade ≥ 3
Vibratory / MusicalHarsh / Blowing
Varies with positionFixed / Does not change

⚠️ Exam Trap: All Diastolic murmurs and all Holosystolic murmurs in children are PATHOLOGIC until proven otherwise. They require an urgent ECHO.

quiz.q: Is a grade 4 systolic murmur in a 5-year-old considered innocent?
quiz.a: No, grade 3 or higher is a red flag for pathology.

title: 💩 COLORECTAL CANCER SCREENING
content:
Canadian Task Force Guidelines (Average Risk).

• Who: Adults aged 50 to 74.
• Test: FIT or gFOBT every 2 years.
• Alternative: Flexible Sigmoidoscopy every 10 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a 1st-degree relative was diagnosed < 60 years old, screening starts at Age 40 (or 10 years before diagnosis) and the test MUST be Colonoscopy every 5 years, not FIT!

quiz.q: What is the preferred screening test for average-risk individuals in Canada?

title: 🎀 BREAST CANCER SCREENING
content:
Current Canadian Guidelines (Average Risk).

• Age 50 - 74: Mammogram every 2 years.
• Age 40 - 49: Not routinely recommended (Shared decision making).

SVG Visual: Mammogram

⚠️ Exam Trap: Clinical Breast Exam (CBE) and Breast Self-Exam (BSE) are NOT recommended for screening in asymptomatic women in Canada (low evidence).

quiz.q: At what age does routine mammography screening stop for average-risk women in Canada?

title: 🍓 KAWASAKI DISEASE
content:
Medium-vessel vasculitis. Think CRASH and Burn.

• C: Conjunctivitis (Non-exudative).
• R: Rash (Polymorphous).
• A: Adenopathy (Cervical).
• S: Strawberry Tongue / Oral changes.
• H: Hand/Foot edema/desquamation.
• Burn: Fever ≥ 5 days.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most feared complication is Coronary Artery Aneurysm. Initial treatment is IVIG + High-dose Aspirin. (Note: This is one of the few times Aspirin is okay in kids!).

quiz.q: What imaging must be done once Kawasaki is suspected?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (TST RULES)
content:
Tuberculin Skin Test (Mantoux) interpretation.

IndurationPositive if...
≥ 5 mmHIV+, Recent contact with TB, Fibrotic CXR.
≥ 10 mmHigh-risk countries, IV drug users, Kids < 4.
≥ 15 mmNo known risk factors.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A previous BCG Vaccine can cause a false positive TST. In these cases, IGRA (blood test) is preferred because it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: How long after the injection should the TST be read?

title: 🤝 INTIMATE PARTNER VIOLENCE (IPV)
content:
Physician role in suspected domestic abuse.

• Approach: Ask directly, validated, and non-judgmental.
• Safety: Assess immediate danger and have a 'Safety Plan'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Unlike child abuse, reporting IPV for competent adults is NOT mandatory in Canada without their consent (Privacy vs. Autonomy). EXCEPTION: If children are in the home and witnessing abuse, you MUST report to Child Services!

quiz.q: What is the priority when a patient discloses IPV?
quiz.a: Assessment of immediate safety and providing resources/safety plan.

title: 🦶 DIABETIC FOOT ULCER (WAGNER)
content:
Leading cause of non-traumatic amputations.

• Pathophysiology: Neuropathy + Ischemia + Trauma.
• Screening: Annual 10g Monofilament test.

SVG Visual: Ulcer Depth

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an ulcer probes to Bone (Probe-to-bone test), the diagnosis is Osteomyelitis until proven otherwise. Next step: MRI or Bone Biopsy.

quiz.q: Which type of neuropathy is most common in Diabetic foot?

title: 🚬 SMOKING CESSATION (CANADIAN OPS)
content:
Pharmacotherapy options in Canada.

• 1st Line: Varenicline (Chantix), Bupropion (Zyban), or NRT (Patch/Gum).
• Combination: Patch + Gum/Inhaler is more effective than either alone.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Varenicline is generally the most effective single agent. Bupropion is a great choice if the patient also has Depression, but avoid it if there is a History of Seizures or Eating Disorders!

quiz.q: What is a major contraindication for Bupropion?
quiz.a: Seizure disorder or Bulimia/Anorexia (lowers seizure threshold).

title: 💉 HIV PEP VS PREP
content:
FeaturePrEP (Pre-Exposure)PEP (Post-Exposure)
IndicationHigh-risk lifestyleAccidental exposure (Needle/Sex)
TimingContinuousWithin 72 hours
DurationOngoing28 Days

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a healthcare worker has a needle-stick injury, PEP must be started as soon as possible (ideally within 2 hours). Do not wait for the source patient's blood results to start the first dose!

quiz.q: What is the maximum time window to start PEP after exposure?

title: ⚖️ ADVANCE DIRECTIVES (SDM)
content:
Hierarchy of Substitute Decision Makers (Ontario example).

1. Guardian (appointed by court).
2. Attorney for personal care.
3. Representative (appointed by CCB).
4. Spouse or Partner.
5. Children / Parents.

⚠️ Exam Trap: An SDM must follow the patient's Known Wishes. If wishes are unknown, they must act in the patient's Best Interest. A spouse cannot override a patient's previously written 'Living Will'.

quiz.q: Who is the default SDM for an unmarried patient with no children?

title: 👶 18-MONTH WELL BABY VISIT
content:
A critical milestone in the Canadian Rourke Baby Record.

• Motor: Walks well, runs, climbs stairs with help.
• Language: 15 - 20 words, points to body parts.
• Social: Symbolic play (feeding a doll).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Not walking by 18 months is a major developmental red flag and requires immediate investigation for GDD or neuromuscular issues.

quiz.q: What is the standard tool used in Canada to screen for Autism at 18 months?
quiz.a: The M-CHAT-R (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers).
